tldr: Across all 13 country buckets, headlines collectively portray the Cockroach Janta Party movement as a youth-led protest wave that met state repression, with police tear gas and baton charges the near-universal visual anchor. Demands for the education minister's resignation circulate most densely in South Asian and African headlines, the political threat to Modi is foregrounded in Indian domestic and diaspora outlets, and only The Conversation surfaces the structural youth unemployment driver that body-text coverage treats as the root cause.

evidence.quote: Indian police bash and gas youth-led 'cockroach' protesters
Young woman injured in police action on ventilator
Indian police fire tear gas as thousands of protesters
will not march again because police will 'hurt youth'
Indian police baton charge protesters marching to parliament
vows to protest until education minister resigns
vows relentless protest until education minister resigns
vows to protest until education minister resigns
Demands PM Modi, Amit Shah's Resignation
India protesters press on after crackdown, pressuring Modi
The youth jobs crisis fuelling India's Cockroach Janta

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline to use physically violent vernacular ('bash and gas') rather than procedural terms like 'baton charge', 'fire tear gas', or 'block' — the sharpest rhetorical register for police action in the corpus, notable for a pan-Asian commentary outlet rather than a wire service.

The only headline in the corpus to frame the story as a government intelligence failure ('Did govt and Delhi Police misread scale') rather than violence or demands — a distinctly self-critical domestic angle that treats the protest as a policing blunder rather than a confrontation or a movement.

The only headline to frame the movement through its identity arc ('started as satire but is serious about change') and label its base as 'Gen Z' — treating the CJP as a generational cultural phenomenon rather than an event-driven protest, a framing absent from every other outlet's title.

bottom_line: Headlines globally advertise India's Cockroach protests as a police-crackdown story, with education-minister resignation demands concentrated in South Asian and African titles, political threat-to-Modi framing in domestic and diaspora outlets, and the structural youth unemployment driver surfacing in just one headline.
